nativeRange.habitat: The Golden Neon Goby is found on coral reefs throughout its range, typically near coral heads and rocky outcroppings in clear oceanic water, from the shallows down to depths of about 50 m.

diet.notes: Primarily carnivorous; generally accepts high-quality frozen meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and finely chopped krill. Has been documented supplementing its diet with small live foods. In the wild, tends to feed on ectoparasites picked from host fish; in captivity it typically adapts well to prepared foods even without a cleaning role.

avoidWith: aggressive fish that may outcompete for food

imageCandidates.notes: Laszlo Ilyes; 1800x1123. Sharp, whole fish on yellow brain coral showing the classic sharknose profile with iridescent lateral stripe and gold head marking; highest resolution and cleanest ID.
Pauline Walsh Jacobson; 960x720. Sharp, whole vibrant individual on orange coral with distinct yellow V head marking and blue-white stripe; light watermark.
Pauline Walsh Jacobson; 960x720. Sharp, whole fish against red sponge with vivid green-gold lateral stripe; strong colour contrast for variety; light watermark.

summary: The Golden Neon Goby is a small Caribbean cleaner fish generally considered beginner-friendly and reef-safe. It tends to pick parasites from other fish in the wild and adapts readily to prepared foods in captivity, making it a peaceful addition to most community reef aquariums.
careNotes: The Golden Neon Goby (Elacatinus evelynae) is also commonly sold as the Sharknose Goby or Gold Neon Goby. It is distinguished by a bright yellow-gold lateral stripe running the length of the body and functions as a cleaner fish in the wild, picking external parasites from larger fish — behavior that may occasionally be observed in the home aquarium as well. The species is generally considered peaceful toward other fish but has been known to show territorial aggression toward conspecifics (other Golden Neon Gobies), particularly in smaller tanks. Keeping groups of seven or more has been reported to reduce this tension in larger, more spacious systems, provided all individuals are introduced at the same time. This species tends to stay close to rockwork and coral heads, darting into crevices when startled. It has been reported to live approximately 12–24 months in captivity, though lifespan can vary considerably with husbandry quality. Stable reef alkalinity in the range of 8–12 dKH is generally advised. Note: the trade name 'Neon Gold Stripe Goby' is sometimes applied to Elacatinus figaro (Barber Goby), a related but distinct species found only off Brazil — buyers should confirm the scientific name when sourcing to avoid mix-ups.
breedingNotes: The Golden Neon Goby has been bred in captivity with some regularity. Pairs form monogamous bonds and deposit adhesive eggs directly onto hard surfaces such as rock or rubble. The eggs have been reported to hatch in approximately 8 days. The newly hatched larvae are free-swimming and require very small live foods — typically rotifers and zooplankton — before they are large enough to accept other prepared items. Captive breeding is considered achievable for dedicated hobbyists, though raising the larvae through their early stages demands attentive feeding and water quality management.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Collection page did not have a dedicated Golden Neon Goby listing; species data sourced from secondary and supplementary sources.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk
additional.notes: Primary data source for water parameters, size, temperament, and breeding notes. Confirmed adult size 4 cm, temperature 23–27°C, salinity 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name Elacatinus evelynae, taxonomy (Gobiidae / Gobiiformes), max size 4.0 cm, and distribution: Western Atlantic — Bahamas and Lesser Antilles to northern coast of South America.

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (10–15 gal / ~38–57 L), temperature 72–78°F (22–26°C), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.020–1.025. Note: product title lists Elacatinus figaro — likely a data-entry error on that retailer's page; scientific name in URL and species description are consistent with E. evelynae husbandry parameters.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn Blog
additional.notes: Additional cross-reference for care level (easy), temperament (peaceful), reef safety, and lifespan (~12–18 months). Corroborates 10-gal minimum for solitary individuals.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been bred in captivity. Pairs lay adhesive demersal eggs on hard surfaces that hatch in approximately 8 days. The resulting larvae require rotifers and zooplankton during their earliest stages. Larval rearing is considered achievable but demands careful attention to feeding and water quality.
